body:
Continuation of #39209, based on [my comment here](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39209#issuecomment-4474816822).

### Problem

The recently merged #39845 adds a pair of text-objects: `al` for "all lines" and `il` for "inside line (line with no leading or trailing whitespace)". These are both useful text-objects, but they don't match well to each other as pairs.

### Expected behavior

- `al` is moved to some other mapping. `ag` and `ae` are both good options imo.
- The current `al` is changed from "all lines" to "the current line, including leading and trailing whitespace, but not including EOL". So while `dd` deletes a line, `dal` would delete the *contents* of a line.
- If we decide we need an "inner text-object" for `ag`/`ae`, we can base this new `ig`/`ie` off of [vim-textobj-entire](https://github.com/kana/vim-textobj-entire)'s `ie`. Otherwise, as [@drowning-cat points out](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39209#issuecomment-4470857980), it might be worth it to have `ag` without a corresponding `ig` (as an exception).

cc @barrettruth @drowning-cat

comment_data.body: What is the actual problem with the current setup? If  al and il 'don't match well', this proposed solution doesn't seem to match well either.
In what way does the proposed solution not match well? `il` would be "inside line", and `al` would be "outside line". I daresay it perfectly matches.
I was thinking of matching pair with the `buffer` text object. But ya `il` and `al` probably mesh well.

I agree, extracting whole-buffer selection into a separate textobject makes perfect sense to me, because selecting the whole line is about as useful as selecting it without leading/trailing whitespace.

Between `ae` and `ag`, `ag` seems better. `ae` not only has a weak mnemonic (see https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39209#issuecomment-4275852825), but it can also easily be associated with the `w`/`b`/`e` motions, so it's probably better to keep it available for future use. On the other hand, it's hard to come up with a meaningful use case for `ag` besides the association with `gg` and `G`.

If this route is taken, the main question is whether `ig` should be mapped at all, since there does not seem to be a strong use case for it. For example, selecting the whole buffer without leading tabs/spaces is not particularly useful (see https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39209#issuecomment-4470754181). Another possible interpretation would be selecting the visible viewport instead of the whole buffer, but that also feels fairly niche.

So the simplest solution is probably to make `ig` and `ag` behave the same for now and call it a day, hoping a better use case for `ig` appears later. It also makes for a good first PR.

If you use  `ig`/`ag`, the whole "g" namespace for text objects is wiped out.

`igl`/`agl` make sense in isolation, but I don't know if that contradicts any plans for using `gl` in normal mode.

> If you use `ig`/`ag`, the whole "g" namespace for text objects is wiped out

I don't think we need a namespace here. We're nowhere near running out of letters for text objects (we only have 11 text-objects currently), and I'm not a fan of having three character text objects.

​I think "running out of letters" is something to take care of. People use many custom text-objects, and there will never be a letter that makes everyone happy. Adding new text-objects is nice, but adding more than necessary just for consistency's sake without being really useful is not something I agree with as a simple user. There are many letters to choose from now, but we have already had many discussions in other topics about "missing namespaces" (using the Alt key because there are no more useful Ctrl mappings, `cr`/`gr` for LSP/refactoring, and more), and I think it is a good idea to be preemptive.

I dont understand why a text object is needed for this usecase. If you want to select the whole buffer just do `gg Shift+v G` which works also in vim.  `vagl` is one char (modifier-key) less. I don't get the benefit.

> The current al is changed from "all lines" to "the current line, including leading and trailing whitespace, but not including EOL". So while dd deletes a line, dal would delete the contents of a line.

In addition to @superatomic proposal, I would suggest that `val` should select the current line, and also surrounding lines that only contains whitespace or are empty lines. Behavior see here: (https://github.com/neovim/neovim/discussions/39886)

> dal would delete the contents of a line.

`shift+vd` should be fine here, I think everybody uses that currently
 In my opinion `dal` should delete the current line, and trailing and leading empty lines (or lines that only contains whitespaces)

I also agree with @mikejmcguirk ... the ig/ag is currently not used, but for me the `g` is kinda of a prefix for custom stuff. (I don't now how to describe my thoughts perfectly).. In normal mode it's used for several stuff, so I would avoid to waste it for something trivial like the whole-buffer-select. I could imagine, that users would like to use the `g` as a prefix for their own textobjects that could have multiple characters.

> I dont understand why a text object is needed for this usecase.

Please read #39209. The main benefit of a text-object is that it does not move the cursor. Compare the current `gqal` to `ggVGgq`. This issue is about renaming `al`, not whether it should exist.

> I would suggest that `val` should select the current line, and also surrounding lines that only contains whitespace or are empty lines.

That doesn't feel like it matches `il` to me.

> > I dont understand why a text object is needed for this usecase.
> 
> Please read [#39209](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39209). The main benefit of a text-object is that it does not move the cursor. Compare the current `gqal` to `ggVGgq`.
> 
> > I would suggest that `val` should select the current line, and also surrounding lines that only contains whitespace or are empty lines.
> 
> That doesn't feel like it matches `il` to me.

To be honest. After you wrote... I saw and tested `gqal` the first time. Now I understand what's going on here. I thought `al` is a new global. I didn't know that it is allready used for formatting. Shame on me :-)

But anyway some thoughts..

As far as I know, the cursor position is either on the start or the end of the visual selection. So I don't understand why the current cursor position should be preserved. It changes anyway. Maybe I don't get your point exactly or the case you want to describe. For example if I do `va{`, the cursor jumps to the end of the selection.

`al` and `il` don't match at all in my suggestion. We have 3 cases, not 2.:
1. select the whole line -> `Shift+v`
2. select the inner text of one line, without trailing and leading whitespaces -> `il`
3. select the line with "around" behaviour (trailing whitespace/wordboundaries like `aw`, but also with leading whitespace boundaries, because right-only boundaries doens't make sense here) -> `al`

> > dal would delete the contents of a line.
> 
> `shift+vd` should be fine here, I think everybody uses that currently

I think there's some confusion here. The proposed `al` would be character-wise, just like `il` is. Notice how `Vd` and `dd` are the same: they delete the line, including the EOL.

> `al` and `il` don't match at all in my suggestion.

Them not matching is the problem we are trying to solve in this PR. Changing `al` to something else which *also* doesn't match is definitely not the way to go.

Very reluctant to split the "l" textobject into 2 different textobjects. There needs to be a strong use-case to occupy a new textobject name.

If the "without whitespace" version of "whole buffer" is useful, let's just define `al` to do that. Maybe it's never useful to copy all the surrounding whitespace.

> Maybe it's never useful to copy all the surrounding whitespace.

Copy - probably yes. Most of my use cases for "buffer textobject" are "change" and "delete", though, which do need to be the actual whole buffer. I'd say that this is a point for a separate textobject, although the frequency of "yank the whole buffer that has leading/trailing blank lines" is rather low for me.

True. Then it hinges on how useful it is to operate on "whole buffer without surround whitespace". If that has high demand then we may need 2 different textobjects. If it doesn't then life is easy and sweet and we can close this :)

(I don't see the "current line including whitespace" use-case important at all, so I'm ignoring it.)

> True. Then it hinges on how useful it is to operate on "whole buffer without surround whitespace". If that has high demand then we may need 2 different textobjects. If it doesn't then life is easy and sweet and we can close this :)

In my experience - not so much. I don't actually remember when I used it. But so is the case for the "inner line" textobject: `dd` / `cc` / `yy` actually do what I'd expect (`cc` also adjusts indent with relevant options enabled), text operators have dedicated line variants (similar to `gcc` and `guu` and such). So not sure how useful my experience here is.

`sailf` (see [mini.surround](https://github.com/nvim-mini/mini.surround)) or something similar feels natural to me, although `Vsaf` works well too.

`vil` + `an` (incremental selection) also works great, although it might be unintended behavior that `Van` doesn't intelligently select parent nodes.

In both cases, priority is still given to selecting a line without leading/trailing spaces.

> `sailf` from [mini.surround](https://github.com/nvim-mini/mini.surround) or something similar feels natural to me, though `Vsaf` works as well.

As well as `sa_f` in this particular case.

In the individual line case, my use case is usually something like I have a function call on one line that I want to pass as a param on another line, so including whitespace in the yank would be counter-productive. For deleting the line's content without removing it, `0D` already works

For the whole buffer, I can see use cases for both. But in terms of, which unwanted UX experience would be the most frustrating, I think it would be yanking the whole buffer, pasting it, then having to manually clean up the leading/trailing newlines. It's also easier to do `Gogg` to expand the visual selection than it is to shrink it back down. Leading/trailing newlines also seems like the less prominent use case given that formatters often remove them.

I actually wish that a standalone period (.) would default to being a linewise text object.

That way, d. could replace dd or d_.

This is how I personally use it. On one hand, as the number of operators grows, repeating the operator becomes less reliable, and you end up needing separate mappings for each operator, which gets tedious. On the other hand, _ is awkward to press. Meanwhile, . has no meaning by itself in Visual mode or Operator-pending mode, and since it already represents the current line as an address, it feels like a very natural fit for this meaning.

To summarize the thoughts I'm seeing in this thread:
- [vim-textobj-entire](https://github.com/kana/vim-textobj-entire)'s `ie` map is way too niche to be useful. If `al` is moved to a different mapping. If we assume that we would move `al` to `ag`, then `ig` should either be unbound or [be equivalent to `ag`](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39893#issuecomment-4490886734).
- It seems we've decided on moving `al` to `ag` if we do end up moving it (if anyone has any other maps that they would prefer, please say so!)
- The concern against moving `al` to a different text-object is that we are introducing is twofold:
  - We don't have infinite letters, and this would be using up `g` in addition to `l`.
  - For `g` specifically, [there is the possibility of making `ag`/`ig` a namespace of text-objects instead of just a single text-object](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39893#issuecomment-4491055560). If this is a concern, we can simply move `ag` to some other map, like `ae`. It won't affect whether we move `al`.
- The proposed new `<op>al` to compliment `<op>il` [can be replicated with `0<op>$`](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39893#issuecomment-4499268904), which could be an argument against adding it. I think this is fine; after all, `<op>il` is the same as `^<op>g_`.

I think to resolve this discussion and move forward, we need to decide if the cost of adding another text-object is worth solving the inconsistency between `al` and `il`, since I think all other concerns are just delaying this decision. _Once_ we've decided that we want to add this, we can then ask more specific questions like "should we use `ag`, or keep that as a future namespace and use something else?".

Hi, I myself have been using the vim-textobj-entire and vim-textob-line for years and would definitely love if their mappings were upstreamed as is. I'm not a fan of the inconsistency between `il` and `al` and don't mind adding an extra text-object if it addresses that.

> I think to resolve this discussion and move forward, we need to decide if the cost of adding another text-object is worth solving the inconsistency between `al` and `il`, since I think all other concerns are just delaying this decision. _Once_ we've decided that we want to add this, we can then ask more specific questions like "should we use `ag`, or keep that as a future namespace and use something else?".

I've had "individual line or whole buffer" on the same key for a long time and it never felt unintuitive or awkward. If the current `al` were moved somewhere else, I'm not sure what would replace it that makes sense.

An alternative idea for whole buffer with/without leading/trailing newlines - `al` without count does not capture them, but `al` with a count also captures extra newlines.

> I've had "individual line or whole buffer" on the same key for a long time and it never felt unintuitive or awkward.

It's the "*inner* individual line", which implies that `al` would be "*outer* individual line". For default binds that are built into Nvim, I strongly believe that these text-objects should actually match up, as it would be a very strange exception that goes against how ai text-objects work. As Justin said at one point, the goal of Nvim is perfection :)

As pointed out by @kuator, there is [prior art to having the `il`/`al` pair be for "inner line" and "outer line"](https://github.com/kana/vim-textobj-line). I independently [arrived at the same exact behavior for `al`](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39209#issuecomment-4474816822) without knowing about `vim-textobj-line`'s existence, which suggests to me that this is the most logical choice for a pair.

> An alternative idea for whole buffer with/without leading/trailing newlines - `al` without count does not capture them, but `al` with a count also captures extra newlines.

It's an interesting idea, but this is very unintuitive; not to mention, `ie` is basically useless.

> It's an interesting idea, but this is very unintuitive; not to mention, `ie` is basically useless.

There is some precedent for something like this. If you have `[ [ [ ] ] ]` with your cursor in the middle of the brackets, and do `v2a]`, it will grab the inner two brackets.

So you could similarly treat "the entire buffer without extra newlines" as the first "level" of the `al` selection.

One thing to correct, `v1al` should still select without trailing newlines (as `v1a]` would only select one pair of brackets). But `v2al` or higher would then select the entire buffer from normal mode.

If you were in visual mode after doing `val`, pressing `al` again (with any count) would then expand the selection to include the extra newlines.

Even if the Vim selection expansion is a bit obscure, incremental selection in general seems to be gaining more traction, so there's a more general baseline intuition to build off of.

I think it's a way to resolve something where, both use cases are valid, but occupying two keys might be too much.

I understand what you're getting at, but I believe it's been decided that "the entire buffer without extra newlines" is very niche (unless you have a usecase?). Because of that, we shouldn't make it the default for `al`.

> but occupying two keys might be too much.

It's not really that much of a cost. Both of them would be useful, so it's not like we're wasting them.

labels.description: Nvim defaults for options, colorscheme/theme, autocmds/events, etc.
labels.name: defaults

labels.description: Need comments/opinions from people familiar with this topic.
labels.name: needs:discussion

title: rename `al` text-object and add a better pair for `il`

type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality
type.name: Enhancement
